query: What are Observables in Angular and how does RxJS fit in?

competitors.label: RxJS Observable Guide
competitors.selectionReason: RxJS is the canonical source for Observable fundamentals, so it is the right baseline for laziness, subscription, teardown, and stream semantics.
competitors.winReason: The RxJS guide is canonical for generic Observable mechanics, but our page is more directly optimized for Angular interview use cases and the follow-up traps around cold versus hot streams, async pipe, and operator choice.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative reference for what an Observable is, how subscription works, and how teardown behaves.
theirGaps: It is less focused on Angular-specific APIs, interview follow-ups, and component usage patterns.

realWorldUseCases: Angular exposes Observables in core APIs (most notably HttpClient, reactive forms valueChanges/statusChanges, and Router.events).
followUpConfusion: Prefer async pipe over manual subscriptions.

realWorldUseCases: The guide explains Observable behavior generically, but it does not anchor the answer in Angular APIs like HttpClient, forms, or router events.
actionableExamples: RxJS docs remain strong on raw Observable creation and subscription examples, so the example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: It explains subscription and teardown well, but it is lighter on Angular-specific follow-ups like async pipe, cold HTTP streams, and common operator-selection questions.

actionableExamples: Add one compact shared-stream example using shareReplay so the Angular side of multiple subscriptions is more concrete.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Basic Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant Angular basics section covering Observables, RxJS operators, and unsubscribe advice in interview language.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd covers the basics cleanly, but our page goes further on Angular API surface, cold versus hot stream behavior, async pipe, and why operator choice matters in production UIs.
theirStrengths: It is concise and interview-friendly, with a simple HttpClient example and operator callouts.
theirGaps: It stays at the beginner level and does not go far into stream-sharing, component teardown patterns, or senior follow-up confusion.

realWorldUseCases: Angular exposes Observables in core APIs (most notably HttpClient, reactive forms valueChanges/statusChanges, and Router.events).
followUpConfusion: Prefer async pipe over manual subscriptions.

realWorldUseCases: The article names HTTP, event handling, and routing, but it does not frame those APIs as separate Angular stream classes with different teardown and sharing behavior.
actionableExamples: Its basic HttpClient subscribe example is concrete enough that the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: The page mentions unsubscribing and operators, but it does not unpack cold versus hot streams or the async-pipe-first Angular mental model.

actionableExamples: Add one side-by-side example contrasting a manual subscribe in a component with the same stream consumed through async pipe.

competitors.label: Angular University RxJs for Beginners
competitors.selectionReason: This is a focused Angular-oriented RxJS tutorial, so it is the strongest long-form reference competitor for Observable fundamentals in Angular.
competitors.winReason: Angular University remains the stronger long-form tutorial, but our page now wins the interview-shaped Angular trade-off and follow-up layers: when RxJS still beats signals, how Router.events fits the model, and how shared streams differ from manual subscribe UI code.
theirStrengths: It is a dedicated long-form tutorial that teaches RxJS concepts with more room for step-by-step examples.
theirGaps: It is less compact than a trivia answer and less tuned for a fast interview-ready summary.

realWorldUseCases: Signals are great for local synchronous state, but Angular still leans on RxJS when you need time, cancellation, concurrency, or multiple async producers.
followUpConfusion: Second Angular example: Router events + shared data

realWorldUseCases: Angular University explains RxJS in Angular app architecture and reactive style, but it is less explicit about the signals-versus-RxJS decision that Angular teams now hit in real code reviews.
actionableExamples: As a long-form beginner guide, it has more room for walk-through examples than our compact answer.
followUpConfusion: It explains stream concepts carefully, but our page now answers the likely next questions around Router.events, shared streams, and manual subscribe versus async pipe more directly.

actionableExamples: Add one explicit invalidation or refresh example around a shared stream so the multicasting example is not only cache-shaped.
